observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry for the stale checks totaling to P856,562.55; and
Municipal Treasurer to (a) send a written notice to the payee to verify the existence of the stale checks and replacement may be issued upon return of the checks; and (b) regularly monitor the outstanding checks and initiate the practice of sending of written notices to the payee at least one month before a check becomes stale, ensuring prompt follow-up and action.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make a representation with the identified contractors to request their submission of final billing to settle the Municipal Government’s obligation and as well as recover the unrecouped advance payments made totaling P189,937.02. If there is a pending valid claim not yet paid to the contractors, the Municipal Accountant should hold the payment of such and apply the unrecouped amount of the advances to contractors.

We recommended that Management to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories as prescribed under Section 51, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.
Direct the Municipal General Services Officer to complete and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for the result of inventory of all supplies account to the Office of the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
Reconcile balances per accounting records and the result of complete physical count of inventories, including the balances reflected in the stock cards.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit a copy of the liquidation report for the P18,000.00 discrepancy to the Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources-Regional Office No. 02 to address the discrepancy, ensuring accurate presentation of accounts in the financial statements of both the BFAR-RO2 and the LGU Palanan. Moreover, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant furnish the audit team a copy of the transmittal received by the BFAR-RO2 as evidence of compliance therein.

We recommended the Management to send written notices to the concerned contractors to inform them on their unclaimed retention monies recorded in the books of the Municipal Government to establish the validity, existence, and correctness of the recorded payable accounts.

We further recommended that Management, upon determination that the concerned contractors are no longer interested in their respective claims, instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to revert back the identified long outstanding payables to the unappropriated surplus, pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 to establish reliability of the accounts balances presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review all accounts affecting the Government Equity under Trust Fund, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and transfer the identified items to the General Fund the Government Equity Account under the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly record all financial transactions using the appropriate account titles based on the actual description of transactions in conformity with the revised chart of accounts in order to permit Management to review its activities according to areas of responsibilities and allow for a clearer and fair presentation of transactions in the financial statements of the Municipality.
Accordingly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify all accounts charged to Other Maintenance of Operating Expenses (Other MOOE) account to the most appropriate accounts in accordance with the prescribed Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit updated report on the status of implementation of the PPAs under LDRRMF including reasons for delay;
Direct the BAC to expedite the procurement process to guarantee the full implementation of the PPAs within the set period to achieve maximum benefits for the constituents of the Municipality and ultimately, serve its purpose of ensuring resiliency of the community during calamities. In event that said PPAs shall be determined to be not viable, the Management should evaluate the same and recommend for reprogramming and prepare and submit an amended LDRRMFIP duly approved by the Local Chief Executive and Sangguniang Bayan; and
The total amount disbursed for the financial assistance be returned by the General Fund to avoid the depletion of the LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that management, through the GAD Focal Point System, submit a written justification for the non-utilization of the full GAD fund and henceforth fully utilize the GAD funds in accordance with the GPB.  We further recommended management to strictly follow standard procedures as laid out in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the COA Orders of Execution (COE) by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to withhold salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowed transactions in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the SDO to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances through the submission of necessary supporting documents. Otherwise, failure of the employee to liquidate his cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause by the Management to withhold of his/her salary or any money due him in accordance with paragraphs 9.3.2 and 9.3.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Ensure that cash advances are fully liquidated within the prescribed periods allowed or after the purpose for which the cash advances were granted has been served to forestall the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Submit their justifications/explanations, including other identified lacking documents and information, if any, for the deficiencies noted in the procurement of welfare goods, for verification;
b) Direct the concerned officials and employees to be meticulous and judicious in the preparation of their Annual Procurement Plan so that procurements made by the Municipality are properly planned and executed, and procurements made through alternative methods do not result in splitting of government contract; and
c) Stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions or contracts on the purchase of goods to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain a fair and equitable contract for the best interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Make a representation with the Accountant of the Department of Social Welfare and Development, Field Office 02, to inquire on the steps to undertake regarding the liquidation of balance of transfer for the RHU repair and submit certification of the Municipal Treasurer that no amount was received for the KALAHI fund transfer for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements of both the DSWD and the Municipality.

Initiate action to reconcile with the Accountant of the Cagayan Valley Center Department of Health (DOH RO 02), as regards the detailed information on the funds recorded as transfers to the LGU in order to correct any discrepancies in the recording of transactions for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements of both the DOH and the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002 in order to forestall the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances. Accordingly, the management should:

Require all concerned employees to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances through the submission of necessary supporting documents. Otherwise, failure of the employee to liquidate his/her cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause by the Management to withhold of his/her salary or any money due him/her in accordance with paragraphs 9.3.2 and 9.3.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and

Discontinue the granting of cash advances to officers and employees for purposes other than for travelling expenses.

1.7    We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  continue  to
         implement the sanctions provided under paragraphs 9.3.2 of the  COA
         Circular 97-002 by requiring the Municipal  Treasurer  to  withhold
         the payment of any money due to the accountable officer.  Also,  we
         recommended that the Management continue to adhere with  the  rules
         in granting,  utilization  and  liquidation  of  cash  advances  as
         prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

2.5    Based on the above observations, we recommended that Management:

          a.     Require  the  Chairman  of  the  Inventory  Committee,  in
             coordination with the Municipal General Services  Officer  and
             Municipal Engineer to submit the Report on Local Road  Network
             of the LGU as shown in Annex A and the Report on the  Physical
             Count of Local Road Network as shown in Annex  C  of  the  COA
             Circular 2015-008. The preparation of the said reports must be
             observed yearly.

          b.    Through  the  Municipal  Accountant  make  the  appropriate
             disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by
             the IPSAS 17.

3.7    We recommended that the Municipal Accountant initiate action  to
         reconcile with the Accountant of the Department of  Social  Welfare
         and Development, Field Office 02, as regards the difference in  the
         reported outstanding balance of funds received  from  the  national
         agency in order to correct any discrepancies in  the  recording  of
         transactions for  a  fair  presentation  of  the  accounts  in  the
         financial statements of both the DSWD and the Municipality.

         Prior to this, the Municipal Accountant should coordinate with  the
         Municipal Treasurer in verifying the recorded disbursements for the
         repair of day care center against their records  such  as  Cash  in
         Bank  Register  and/or  Cashbooks   and   prepare   the   necessary
         Utilization Report duly approved by the Local Chief Executive to be
         submitted to the DSWD, FO2.

4.8    We highly recommended that the Municipal Accountant  immediately
         draw a journal entry to record the receipt of reverted  funds  from
         the Provincial Government of Isabela to reflect a fair presentation
         of balances in the financial statements of the Municipal Government
         of Palanan. Accordingly, the journal entry should be:
         Debit         Due to LGUs                             1,823,692.67
         Credit        Government Equity                  1,823,692.67
                       To correct the entry made on the receipt of  reverted
                 funds.


         Also, we recommended that the Management revert the returned  funds
         to General Fund, since the intended purpose did not  push  through,
         to  provide  additional  resources  to  be  appropriated  for   the
         programs, projects and activities of the Municipality

5.6    We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  review  all  accounts
         affecting the Government  Equity  under  Trust  Fund,  prepare  the
         necessary adjusting entries and transfer the  identified  items  to
         the General Fund the Government  Equity Account under the Trust
         Fund.

6. In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:

          1.      The  Local  Finance  Committee  make  a  systematic   and
             realistic forecasting of revenue  taking  into  account  prior
             years’ experience and statistics as aids in setting targets.

          2.    The Municipal Treasurer and other Departments  involved  in
             generating revenues endeavor to meet its targets and intensify
             all collection efforts. Periodic  review  should  be  made  by
             concerned officials to ascertain whether collection target  is
             attained. The reasons for the shortfall in collections  should
             be analyzed and alternative actions should be looked  into  in
             order to realize projections.

observations.recommendation:
8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue  to  prepare
   the monthly  Bank  Reconciliation  Statements  for  all  its  bank
   accounts and thoroughly reconcile bank  balances  against  general
   ledger balances in order to have a fair presentation of  the  cash
   account in the financial statements of the Municipality.

14. We highly recommended that the Acting  Municipal  Treasurer,
   together with the Revenue Collection Clerks,  immediately  produce
   the missing funds totaling ₱325,223.99 and deposit the  same  with
   the bank account of LGU Palanan, Isabela. This, without  prejudice
   to  the  institution  of  administrative  and/or  criminal  action
   against him/them for malversation  of  public  funds  pursuant  to
   Article 217 of the Revised Penal Code. Moreover,  the  payment  of
   any money due him/them or his/their estate should be  withheld  to
   be applied in satisfaction of the indebtedness pursuant to Section
   37 of PD 1445.

15. We further recommended that  the  Acting  Municipal  Treasurer  be
   immediately relieved from his collection functions until he  shall
   have fully restituted his cash shortage.

16.  Likewise,  we  recommended  that  the   Local   Chief   Executive
   immediately issue  an  order  to  all  accountable  officers  with
   unremitted collections to remit all their collections, discontinue
   their collection functions and cease from collecting  taxes,  dues
   and fees for the Municipality of Palanan.

9. We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the  following  courses
   of action:

 a. Draw the  appropriate  journal  entry  voucher  to  record  the
    transactions affecting the Due from  Other  Funds  and  Due  to
    Other Funds Accounts as follows:


    Special Education Fund Books


    (DR) Cash in bank           1-01-02-010           71,181.52
    (CR) Due from Other Funds   1-03-04-010           71,181.52


    To record the transfer of Municipal share  from  collection  or
    Real Property Tax-Additional  for  the  months  of  October  to
    December 2019 from General Fund



































































    Economic Development Fund under General Fund


    (DR) Due from Other Funds   1-03-04-010      2,520,612.14
    (CR) Subsidy from Other Funds 4-03-02-040         2,520,612.14


    To record the fund transfer due to EDF the  month  of  December
    2019 erroneously deposited to TF

 b. Likewise, eliminate or close the  reciprocal  accounts  in  the
    Consolidated Financial Statements to present fairly the  actual
    financial condition of the Municipal Government of  Palanan  as
    at December 31, 2019.

 c. Henceforth, exercise due care in recording and classifying  the
    Municipality’s  financial  transactions  to  ensure  that   the
    balances of all accounts are correct and accurate.

5. We recommended the  Local  Chief  Executive  to  (1)  require  the
   Chairman of the Inventory  Committee,  in  coordination  with  the
   Municipal General Services Officer and Municipal Engineer  to  (a)
   complete the Inventory of all Local Roads and Road Map  and Local
   Road Network Property Cards; and (b) submit the  Report  on  Local
   Road Network of the LGU as shown in Annex A of COA Circular  2015-
   008. The preparation of the Report of Local Road Network  must  be
   observed yearly; (2) create a sub-committee  or  group  under  the
   Municipal Inventory  Committee  that  will  primarily  handle  the
   inventory of local road network system; (3) direct  the  Municipal
   Accountant to  provide  adjusting  journal  entries  to  recognize
   changes upon completion of  the  required  inventory  reports  and
   reconciliation  with  the  Registry  of   Public   Infrastructure.
   Further, depreciation  shall  be  provided  accordingly;  and (4)
   direct the Municipal Accountant to make the appropriate disclosure
   in the Notes to Financial Statements as Illustrated in Annex B  of
   the said COA Circular.

10. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  immediately  draw  a
   journal entry to record the receipt of funds from source  agencies
   as Due to NGAs to reflect a fair presentation of balances  in  the
   financial statements  of  the  Municipal  Government  of  Palanan.
   Accordingly, the journal entry should be:

          Debit Cash in Bank                xx
                Credit     Due to NGAs                  xx
          To record the receipt of funds from DBM and DILG.

8. In this regard,  we  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant
   initiate action to reconcile with the Accountant of the Department
   of Health (DOH), RO2 as regards the  difference  in  the  reported
   outstanding balance of funds received from the national agency  in
   order  to  correct  any  discrepancies   in   the   recording   of
   transactions for a  fair  presentation  of  the  accounts  in  the
   financial statements of both the DOH and the Municipality.

9. Prior to this, the Municipal Accountant should coordinate with the
   Municipal Treasurer in verifying the  recorded  disbursements  for
   the improvement/extension of the laboratory building against their
   records such as Cash in Bank Register and/or Cashbooks and prepare
   the necessary Utilization Report duly approved by the Local  Chief

8. We recommended that the Management direct  the  Local  Development
   Council  and  the  Municipal  Planning  and  Development   Office,
   together with the concerned implementing offices, in  coordination
   with the Bids  and  Awards  Committee  to  vigorously  pursue  the
   implementation of the projects  to  be  implemented  in  a  timely
   manner in order to achieve  the  objectives  in  providing  socio-
   economic benefits to the intended beneficiaries.

6. We recommended that Management strictly comply with the guidelines
   prescribed in the DLG-DBM Joint Circular that  priority  programs,
   projects and activities funded  under  the  20%  Development  Fund
   should partake of the nature of investment of capital  expenditure
   and exclude administrative and regular operating expenditures.

13. We recommended that  the:  a)  Local  Chief  Executive  (i)  issue
   termination of contract to the erring contractor whose project has
   incurred a negative slippage of ten percent (10%) or more  in  the
   completion of the work pursuant to Item 2 of Annex I of the IRR of
   RA 9184 on the Guidelines on Termination of  Contracts;  and  (ii)
   cause the forfeiture of the performance security of the contractor
   pursuant to 39.3 of the same law; b) Bids and Awards Committee (i)
   facilitate the preparation and issuance of  a  Blacklisting  Order
   upon termination of contracts due to  default  of  the  contractor
   pursuant  to  the   Uniform   Guidelines   for   Blacklisting   of
   Manufacturers,   Suppliers,   Distributors,    Contractors,    and
   Consultants; (ii) strictly impose liquidated damages  against  the
   contractor pursuant to Section 8.1 of the Contract  Implementation
   Guidelines for the Procurement  of  Infrastructure  Projects;  and
   (iii) henceforth, make sure that all infrastructure  projects  are
   strictly monitored in accordance  with  plans  and  schedules  of
   implementation so  that  available  actions  or  remedies  can  be
   imposed against erring contractors; and c) Local  Chief  Executive
   notify in writing the Department of Health on the  status  of  the
   implementation of the  Construction  of  Palanan  Level  II  Water
   System in accordance with the  Project  Memorandum  Agreement  and
   coordinate with the  source  agency  on  other  possible  remedial
   actions to be taken thereon in order to expedite the completion of
   the said project.

9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  ensure  that  claims
   for payment of  plane  fares  of  all  qualified  individuals  are
   supported with complete documentation. Require all  officials  and
   employees from the national government agencies who  are  assigned
   or not assigned thereat to submit the duly approved travel  orders
   from their respective agencies concerned to  guarantee  that  only
   official travels are allowed to be paid by LGU Palanan. Otherwise,
   all travels without  the  duly  approved  Travel  Order  shall  be
   considered personal and should be borne by  the  official/employee
   or individual concerned.

10. We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare  and  Development
   Officer and  Municipal  Accountant  ensure  that  all  claims  and
   payrolls for financial  assistance  are  supported  with  complete
   documentation. Likewise, the Disbursing Officer  should  ascertain
   that all payrolls for financial assistance are  properly  received

observations.reason:
Item #1 | For Implementation on CY 2019 | Partially Implemented
The amount of ₱1,950,698.00 that was allocated for GAD-related activities remained unutilized at year-end, hence, the benefits were not fully attained to the prejudice of the constituents which is not in consonance with COA Circular 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 and Section 4.4 of DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular 2004-01 dated April 5, 2004.

We recommended that the Management maximize the use of the budget allocated for GAD activities in order to fully advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare of women that will protect and fulfill their rights as prescribed under COA Circular 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 and Section 4.4 of DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular 2004-01 dated April 5, 2004.

Item #2 |  | Fully Implemented.
The management failed to set up and maintain a GAD Database as required under 4.0B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, thus, affecting the proper implementation of Magna Carta of Women (MCW).

We recommended that the Management establish and maintain a GAD Database to ensure and sustain the LGUs critical consciousness in supporting gender and development, women’s empowerment and responding to gender issues as prescribed in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

Item #3 | The LGU is currently updating its CBMS Program which will serve as a basis for the establishment of GAD Database. | Partially Implemented.

             required under COA Cir No. 96-010  to  enable  the  Auditor  to
             conduct its inspection, if feasible.

10.7    In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:


          a. The Local Finance Committee make  a  systematic  and  realistic
             forecasting  of  revenue  taking  into  account  prior   years’
             experience and statistics as aids in setting targets.


          b. The Municipal  Treasurer  and  other  Departments  involved  in
             generating revenues endeavor to meet its targets. Intensify all
             collection efforts. Periodic review should be made by concerned
             officials to ascertain whether collection target are  attained.
             The reasons for the shortfall in collections should be analyzed
             and alternative actions should  be  looked  into  in  order  to
             realize projections.

11.8    We recommended that the agency focal person prepare  GAD  plans
          that would specifically address the current  issues  and  concerns
          affecting the community  in  support  of  the  attainment  of  the
          objectives of the GAD  program  of  the  government.  Furthermore,
          identify the Management’s PPAs to be included  in  the  GPB  which
          will give priority and promote GAD issues and concerns  especially
          the rights of the women.

4. We recommended that the Management maximize the use of the  budget
          allocated for GAD activities in  order  to  fully  advocate  women
          empowerment, promote gender equality and improve  the  welfare  of
          women that will protect and fulfill  their  rights  as  prescribed
          under COA Circular 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 and  Section  4.4
          of DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular 2004-01 dated April 5, 2004.

6. We recommended that the Management establish and  maintain  a  GAD
          Database to ensure and sustain the LGUs critical consciousness  in
          supporting  gender  and  development,  women’s   empowerment   and
          responding to gender issues  as  prescribed  in  PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
          Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

4. We recommended the following:


          a.  Management  particularly  the  HRM   Officer   and   Municipal
             Accountant  strictly  comply  with  the  existing   rules   and
             regulations stated above in the preparation and approval of the
             payrolls to avoid  sanctions  that  may  be  imposed  for  non-
             compliance thereof.


          b. In the event that the total authorized  deductions  reduce  the
             net take home  pay  to  less  than  P4,000.00,  the  authorized
             deductions for BIR, GSIS,  HDMF  and  PHIC  shall  enjoy  first
             preference, those deductions for mutual  benefits  association,
             thrift banks and  savings  and  loan  association  shall  enjoy
             second preference and so forth. The other deductions  that  are
             not accommodated during the pay period should already  be  paid
             directly by the employee concerned to the loaning institution.


          c.   The HRMO exercise diligence in  certifying  the  monthly  net
             take home pay of employees and provide  emergency  measures  to
             assuage  the  situation  of  those  who  have  been  negatively
             affected by their loan deductions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management require  the  Accounting  Office  to
record ALL LBP checks issued in the books of accounts  with  emphasis  on
those  already  negotiated  with  the  Landbank  and  that  the  covering
disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents be submitted
to the Office of the Auditor for final disposition in audit and to  avoid
audit  suspensions.  Avoid  or  stop  drawing  of   checks   before   the
disbursement  vouchers  are  prepared  and  processed  and  observe   the
procedures in processing  payments  of  claims  to  avoid  incurrence  of
missing disbursement vouchers and checks  which  ultimately  resulted  to
unrecorded checks.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Office of
the Municipal Accountant  to  prepare  the  Monthly  Bank  Reconciliation
Statements for all accounts/funds of the Municipal  Government  to  check
the correctness of the balances of Cash in Bank appearing in the books of
accounts and in the Cashbook maintained by  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to
determine any unrecorded  checks  already  negotiated  in  the  bank  and
furnish copies of the said statements to the Office of  the  Auditor  for
verification.

We recommended that the Local  Chief  Executive  require  the  OIC,
Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all collection turned over  to  him
and to refrain from accommodating disbursements for payment out from the
collections. A petty cash fund should be established  to  cover  payments
for petty operating expenses in order to forestall using of collection.

     Adhere strictly with the provisions  of  Items  8  and  28  of  COA
Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer to record all checks drawn in his name for cash advances in his
cashbooks and that checks drawn for  cash  advances  shall  be  equal  to
payrolls and special disbursements to be paid and be recorded at the time
these cash advances are granted.

We recommended that the OIC, Municipal Treasurer and the  OIC,
Municipal Accountant reconcile regularly the balances  appearing  in  the
Cashbooks and the General Ledgers in compliance with COA  Memorandum  No.
84-373 and Section 6.3 of COA Circular No  97-002  and  to  arrive  at  a
correct balance of the cash accounts.

We recommended that management require the OIC, Municipal
Accountant to  strictly  monitor  the  settlement  of  unliquidated  cash
advances. Subsequently, officials and  employees  with  outstanding  cash
advances who get separated from the service should not  be  cleared  from
money/property accountability unless liquidations are made.

     We also recommended that the Chief Executive require the  Municipal
Treasurer to increase amounts to be deducted from  the  payrolls  of  the
employees concerned in order to fasten settlement of liquidation of their
cash advances and that at the end of the year,  such  cash  advances  are
fully liquidated.

     We recommended that management adhere strictly on the provisions of
cash advances pursuant to Section 89 of PD  1445  and  Item  5.8  of  COA
Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that management require the  Accounting  Office  and
the General Services Office to reconcile their respective records.  Also,
require the  Accounting  Office  to  maintain  the  Property,  Plant  and
Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) for each category of plant,  property  and
equipment, Real Property Ledger Cards (RPLC) for  land  and  the  General
Services Office to maintain Property  Cards  per  category  of  property,
plant and equipment.

We recommended that the OIC, Municipal Accountant only remits  what
has been deducted in the  payrolls  to  avoid  over-remittances  of  loan
repayments and  other  mandatory  deductions.  We  also  recommended  the
reconstruction of subsidiary ledger for each employee  to  determine  the
amount due from them and immediately collect, if not, deduct  the  amount
from their salaries.

We recommended that the Accounting Office  exert  extra  effort  to
locate  the  documents  to  substantiate  these  receivable  and  payable
accounts, to review and analyze the details  of  these  accounts  and  to
maintain subsidiary ledgers for each account. Management should work  out
the request for authority to write off these receivables from the books.

We recommended that the unexpended balances of  the LDRRMF  should
accrue to a Special Trust Fund and be provided with adequate  funding  or
cash pursuant to Section 21, Paragraph 3 of the Republic Act No. 10121.


     We also recommended that a separate bank account be opened for this
fund to ensure that the cash allocated for this  fund  are  in  tact  and
assurance that cash are available in cases of disasters.

We recommended that management  require  the MDRRMC  to  regularly
prepare a Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same  to
the COA Auditor. Likewise, require the MDRRMC  to  make  the  report  for
public notice through publication and posting in conspicuous  places  and
its website. Also, require the  Accountant  Office  to  comply  with  the
reportorial requirements on the Accounting and Reporting  Guidelines  for
the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management  Fund  (LDRRMF)  of  the
LGU, National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (NDRRMF)  given
to LGUs and Receipts from Other Sources.

We recommended that management require that all disbursements  must
be adequately supported  with  the  required  documents  to  avoid  audit
suspensions  and/or  disallowances.   The   Commission   on   Audit   had
issue/circularize COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June  14,  2012  titled
“Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions” for
ready reference.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive   require   the
Accounting  Office,  Treasury  Office  and  other  concerned  offices  to
strictly adhere with the requirements  on  the  submission  of  financial
statements and other financial  reports  as  well  as  paid  disbursement
vouchers and payrolls pursuant to Section 7.2 (a) and (b) of COA Circular
2009-006.

     We also recommended  that  management  require  the  all  concerned
offices to follow the proper steps  in  processing  vouchers/payrolls  as
well as recording and payment or release of claims to avoid  missing  and
unsubmitted vouchers/payrolls.
